appliedAdds.reason: In 'ekaṁ samayaṁ' eka functions as an indefinite article: 'on a certain occasion'. Contextually true and distinct from the bare numeral 'one'.
The adverbial accusative 'ekaṁ samayaṁ' is standardly rendered 'once / at one time'; DPD attests this reading and it is distinct from the numeral sense.

appliedAdds.sense: name of a town in the Kuru country
appliedAdds.reason: Contextually true and adds real content beyond the bare 'Place name' gloss: it identifies the referent as a town and locates it in the Kuru country, exactly what the phrase 'kurūnaṁ nigamo' asserts.

appliedAdds.sense: name of the people of Kuru; Kurus
appliedAdds.reason: The genitive plural here refers to the Kuru people ('a town of the Kurus'); this sense identifies who the Kurus are (a people/tribe), which the existing purely grammatical glosses ('of the Kurus') do not convey. The alternative homonym 'name of a country' is excluded since the plural here denotes the people, not the country.

appliedAdds.sense: (of breathing) for a short time; shallowly
appliedAdds.reason: DPD's breath-specific gloss; 'shallowly / for a short time' is contextually exact here and adds content (shallowness, duration) not covered by the bare cores 'short/quickly/lightly'.

appliedAdds.sense: (of breathing) for a short time; shallowly
appliedAdds.reason: Same as as1: the DPD breath-context gloss is the precise sense in this phrase and genuinely distinct from the existing core glosses.

appliedAdds.sense: monk; monastic; mendicant; fully ordained monk; lit. beggar
appliedAdds.reason: Contextually true and adds genuinely distinct content beyond core 'a monk': 'mendicant' (standard modern translation, e.g. Sujato) and the literal etymology 'beggar' (from bhikkhati). Same referent, richer required reference content.

DPD's contextual gloss for digham in breathing passages: accusative used adverbially, so 'breathing long' = breathing for a long time / deeply. Distinct from the bare adjective 'long' and matches the golden's own [Accusative Adverbial] tooltip.
Same as aw5: DPD's contextual adverbial gloss for digham of breathing, genuinely distinct from the adjective 'long'.

appliedAdds.sense: (of breathing) for a short time; shallowly
appliedAdds.reason: DPD's explicitly breath-contextual gloss: accusative adverbial 'breathing short' = for a short time / shallowly. Genuinely distinct from the bare adjective 'short' and exactly matches this phrase's usage.

appliedAdds.sense: (of breathing) for a short time; shallowly
appliedAdds.reason: Same as aw17: DPD's breath-contextual adverbial gloss, distinct from the bare adjective 'short'.
Distinct dative reading of assa (pana + assa): mindfulness 'is established FOR him', which is the natural construal with paccupaṭṭhitā hoti; the existing core sense only gives the genitive 'his'.

The DPD sense for anissita in this context; 'detached/disengaged' adds real content beyond the existing 'independent/not dependent' core senses, matching the commentarial gloss (not dependent on craving and views).

appliedRemoves.reason: Wrong homonym in this context: in 'sattānaṁ visuddhiyā' the word is the genitive plural of satta 'being' (for the purification of beings), not the numeral satta 'seven'. A numerical reading is factually wrong here.

appliedTooltips.to: Points forward to the discourse about to be recited
appliedTooltips.reason: In the opening formula 'evaṁ me sutaṁ', evaṁ is cataphoric: it announces the teaching that follows (as the golden's own core nuance 'Refers to what follows' states). 'Points back to the occasion' asserts the opposite direction and contradicts the core sense.

appliedTooltips.to: Like the "e-" augment in the Greek aorist (Sanskrit uses "a-")
appliedTooltips.reason: The Greek aorist augment is e- (epsilon, as in élusa), not a-; the a- augment is the Sanskrit/Pāli form. The tooltip teaches a real Indo-European cognate feature but attributes the wrong vowel to Greek.

appliedTooltips.to: [Compound] pa + √sthā: to stand (also analyzed as sati + upaṭṭhāna, "attending")
appliedTooltips.reason: Wrong prefix: paṭi + √sthā would yield patiṭṭhāna (Skt pratiṣṭhāna), not paṭṭhāna. The attested derivations are pa + ṭhāna ('setting up, foundation') or sati + upaṭṭhāna ('attending', cf. Skt smṛtyupasthāna).

skipped.item: attending mindfully; being present with mindfulness
